name: Pluggable Modes and Multi-Session
description: Pluggable domain modes, multi-session support, and a non-blocking question mechanism for the Ralph framework.

tasks.description: Generalize the base framework. seed.md, planner.md, ralph.md, and prd.md contain code-specific language (TDD, commits, diffs, test infrastructure, code review, 'tests pass'). The base framework should be domain-neutral — principles that apply whether you're writing code, preparing taxes, or conducting research. Code-specific content needs to move out so a mode layer can own it. Perspectives that are inherently code-specific (implementer, code-cleaner, code-reviewer, explorer) also need to move out of the base. Perspectives that are already general (architect, planner, design-reviewer, spec-reviewer, qa-engineer) stay in the base but may need light edits to remove code-flavored language. The planner's 'Common patterns' section currently lists code pipelines — those patterns should come from the active mode, not be hardcoded. See ralph-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/001/context.md for a file-by-file audit of what's code-specific vs general.
tasks.outcome: Base framework files read naturally for any domain. No code-specific terminology in the base layer. All code-mode content is preserved, just relocated into a separate layer.
tasks.verification: Read seed.md, planner.md, ralph.md, prd.md as if you're building a tax preparation pipeline. Nothing references code, tests, commits, or programming concepts. The base perspectives directory contains only domain-neutral roles.

tasks.description: Create the mode system and the 'code' mode. A mode adds domain-specific perspectives, principles (a seed addendum), and pipeline patterns to the base framework. The first mode is 'code' — it contains everything extracted from task 001 plus the code-specific perspectives. The mode system should be simple file layering: just a directory with the right files that get read when the mode is active. Not a plugin architecture, not a registry, not dynamic loading. Ralph reads the PRD's 'mode' field and loads the mode's files on top of the base. The planner sees base roles + mode roles when composing pipelines. Creating a new mode means adding a directory — no framework code changes. A PRD with no mode or a missing mode still works using only base roles. See ralph-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/002/context.md for proposed directory structure and open questions.
tasks.outcome: A 'code' mode exists with all code-specific content. Ralph loads mode files based on the PRD's mode field. The planner composes pipelines from base + mode roles. The PRD template has a 'mode' field. Creating a new mode is just adding files — no other changes needed.
tasks.verification: An existing code PRD with mode 'code' produces identical behavior to today. A PRD with no mode works with only base roles. Someone could create a 'research' mode by following the code mode's pattern without reading any docs beyond the mode directory itself.

tasks.description: Multi-session support. Currently, `ralph-start.sh` from a second terminal intercepts the existing Docker session (`docker start -ai` reattaches). And two `ralph-loop.sh` instances on the same worktree cause git conflicts. Users need 2+ concurrent Ralph sessions against the same project — e.g., parallel PRDs. The Docker terminal problem is trivially solved with `docker exec`. The git isolation problem needs worktrees or clones so each session has its own working tree. The parallel-agents research doc (ralph-context/designs/parallel-agents-research.md) has prior analysis — full clones recommended for Docker, worktrees for local. The solution should be simple enough to explain in one paragraph.
tasks.outcome: A user can launch a second Ralph session against the same project without disrupting the first. Each session has its own git working tree. The workflow is documented and easy.
tasks.verification: Start two sessions against the same project. Run different PRDs in each. Both complete without git conflicts or Docker session hijacking. The setup took under 2 minutes and no special knowledge.

tasks.description: Non-blocking question mechanism. When a subagent hits genuine ambiguity about intent or requirements that it can't resolve from context, it should be able to ask the human — without blocking other tasks. The question should be written to the task's context directory so it persists. Ralph should park that task and keep dispatching everything else. When Ralph runs out of dispatchable work (everything is complete, blocked, or waiting on answers), it surfaces all accumulated questions to the human in batch. The human answers, Ralph writes answers to task context, and re-dispatches those tasks. This must be opt-in per PRD — sometimes the human wants fully autonomous execution with no questions. A PRD that disallows questions means agents must make their best judgment call or block. See ralph-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/004/context.md for mechanism brainstorming and open questions.
tasks.outcome: Agents can ask questions that don't stall the pipeline. Questions accumulate while other work continues. The human answers in batch when Ralph has nothing else to dispatch. PRDs control whether questions are allowed.
tasks.verification: Run a PRD with questions enabled. One task asks a question. Other tasks continue to completion. Ralph surfaces the question only after exhausting other work. Answer it, task resumes. Run a PRD with questions disabled. Agent that would have asked instead makes a judgment call and continues.

tasks.description: Rename the project from 'ralph-docker' to 'orca'. The `/ralph` skill name and prose references to the ralph loop/process stay. Everything else that's infrastructure/machinery-flavored becomes 'orca': `ralph-context/` → `orca-context/`, `.ralph/` → `.orca/`, infra scripts `ralph-{start,loop,once,reset,clone}.sh` → `orca-*.sh`, `RALPH_PROMPT.md` → `ORCA_PROMPT.md`, Docker image tag `ralph-claude` → `orca-claude`. Also delete `.ralph-tasks/` entirely (ghost directory — nothing in `framework/` or `.claude/skills/` references it; everything writes to `ralph-context/tasks/`). Handle `ralph-logs/` based on whether bash-loop mode survives. This repo is self-hosted so it's also its own first upgrade case. See ralph-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/005/context.md for the full inventory.
tasks.outcome: Repo uses 'orca' for infrastructure/machinery and 'ralph' only for the loop process and `/ralph` skill name. Self-hosting still works end-to-end after the rename. README, CLAUDE.md, and docs are updated to match.
tasks.verification: `grep -r ralph` returns only intentional hits: process prose, `/ralph` skill path, `ralph-loop.sh` references that are actually describing the loop process. All infra scripts, dotfile dirs, log dirs, prompt file, and Docker image tag use orca. The framework still installs cleanly into itself and a `/ralph` run of an existing PRD works.

tasks.description: Make Orca easy to install into any project. Vision: from a fresh or existing project, the user tells Claude 'install Orca from <github URL>' and a skill fetches the framework and lays down all files, configs, and scaffolds — no manual steps, no separate installer script run. Re-running the install is safe: it upgrades the framework files, leaves user content alone, and migrates pre-rename installs (projects with `.ralph/`, `ralph-context/`, etc.) to the new orca-* paths. Includes git hook wiring, `.gitignore` additions, applying the recommended Claude settings template (see task 007), and `orca-context/` scaffolding. This task is on the larger side — architect should consider splitting during pipeline composition if the chosen mechanism expands scope (e.g., fresh install as one task, upgrade + rename migration as a follow-up). See ralph-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/006/context.md for mechanism options, upgrade/migration strategy, and open questions.
tasks.outcome: A user can install Orca into any git repo via a single instruction inside Claude Code, using current (April 2026) skill-install patterns. Re-running safely upgrades framework files. Re-running against a pre-rename `ralph-*` project migrates it to `orca-*` without losing user content. The existing `install.sh` is deleted.
tasks.verification: Install into a fresh empty git repo — all framework files, skills, settings, scaffolds, hooks, and gitignore entries are in place. Install into a pre-existing `ralph-*` installation — it migrates cleanly to `orca-*` with no lost user content. Install into an already-up-to-date project — it's a no-op with a clear report. The whole install takes under 30 seconds and requires no shell commands from the user.

tasks.description: Move the Claude Code settings template to a well-organized spot in `framework/` (currently parked at `framework/template.claude.settings.json`, should probably join `framework/templates/prd.json`). Wire the install skill to offer the recommendation, let the user pick destination (project vs user level) and whether to apply, and honor the choice. Not mandatory, never silent.
tasks.outcome: Settings template lives in a sensible location under `framework/`. Install skill offers the settings with context, user picks destination + apply/skip, outcome matches their choice.
tasks.verification: Install offers settings, user sees what they're accepting, declining leaves no trace, accepting lands them where the user chose without clobbering existing values.

tasks.description: Final cleanup after the rename (005), the new install flow (006), and settings move (007) have landed. Sweep the repo for any remaining stale references, ghost concepts, or documentation that contradicts the new state: README.md gets a full rewrite to match the Orca identity and current install story; CLAUDE.md updated for renamed paths and removed concepts (`.ralph-tasks/`, maybe `ralph-logs/`); `docs/structure.md` and other docs updated; stale references in `ralph-context/tasks/` (old PRD context files) flagged or left alone based on their historical value. Also remove anything else the preceding tasks didn't already clean up.
tasks.outcome: The repo reads as a coherent 'Orca' project. README accurately describes what Orca is, how to install it, and how to use it. No docs reference paths or concepts that no longer exist. No ghost directories mentioned in guidance.
tasks.verification: Read the README end-to-end: every install step, path, and concept matches reality. `grep -r ralph` returns only intentional hits (process prose, `/ralph` skill path). No references to `.ralph-tasks/` anywhere in current docs. A newcomer following the README can install and use Orca without hitting stale instructions.
